title: Pavlova
subtitle: Large meringue with a marshmallow centre, cream, and fruit
excerpt: A large meringue with a crisp exterior and a soft, marshmallow interior, topped with whipped cream and fresh fruit. The vinegar and cornflour are not optional: they produce the characteristic soft centre that makes pavlova distinct from ordinary meringue.
sourceNotes: Pavlova was named after the Russian ballerina Anna Pavlova during her 1926 tour of Australasia. Both Australia and New Zealand claim its invention. The recipe is widely documented in Australian and New Zealand household cookbooks from the 1930s; the earliest reliable print version appears in New Zealand. The addition of vinegar and cornflour to produce the soft marshmallow centre is the defining characteristic. Pre-1928 meringue bases without these additions exist; the pavlova form is a 20th-century development.

content.text: Pavlova is not a meringue that got lucky. The
make it what it is. Leave them out and you have a meringue. The exterior should be dry and slightly cracked; the interior should yield to a spoon like a very thick marshmallow.
Ensure the bowl and whisk are completely clean and dry. Any trace of fat prevents the egg whites whipping. Separate the eggs with care; even a drop of yolk will stop the whites forming peaks.

Preheat the oven to 130°C conventional. Draw a 22 cm circle on a sheet of baking paper and turn it over on the tray so the pencil is underneath. Whisk the egg whites in a large clean bowl until they hold stiff peaks. Add the caster sugar a tablespoon at a time, whisking well after each addition. Continue until the meringue is very thick, glossy, and holds stiff, firm peaks; the sugar should be fully dissolved. Rub a small amount between your fingers; it should feel smooth, not gritty. Sprinkle over the cornflour, vinegar, and vanilla and fold in gently with a large spoon.

Spoon the meringue onto the circle, building up the sides to create a slight well in the centre. Bake for 1 hour 15 minutes until the outside is dry and just tinged cream, not white. Switch the oven off and leave the pavlova inside, door closed, until the oven is completely cold. This takes at least 1 hour. Do not open the oven during baking or cooling; sudden temperature changes crack the shell. Once cold, the exterior should be dry and slightly crisp. Store in a cool dry place until needed.

Transfer the pavlova carefully to a serving plate. Spread the whipped cream over the top and into the well. Arrange the fruit generously over the cream. Serve within 2 hours; the cream will gradually soften the meringue shell.
